title: A bill by Senator Dave Cortese allowing judges to review life-without-parole and death penalty sentences for people who have been incarcerated for at least 20 years cleared its fir
prompt_preview:
[1 chunks] Task: Extract all distinct claims about AI or emerging technology in this press release. Do NOT assume a fixed number.
Output JSON only matching the provided schema.
Return concise, standalone claim_texts that could be tested against scientific literature. Exclude purely procedural statem

claims.claim_text: SB 94 would save the state hundreds of millions of dollars each year by giving elder inmates the right to judicial review.
The recidivism rate for the elderly inmate population is zero.
Research shows there is little risk for elderly individuals to re-offend or recidivate upon release.
For individuals previously sentenced to life without parole who were granted a commutation and released, the recidivism rate is zero percent.
The majority of people serving a life without parole sentence are classified as low risk.
88% of people serving life without parole have been assessed with the lowest risk score on CDCR’s California Static Risk Assessment tool.
On average, it costs California a little more than $106,000 per year to keep someone in prison.
The figure for the cost of imprisoning inmates doubles or triples for inmates over 55 or older with chronic health issues.
Approximately 820 people would be eligible for sentencing review under SB 94.
Nearly 99 percent of those eligible for sentencing review are over the age of 50.
Over 90 percent of those eligible for sentencing review have served over 30 years.
